file_name: Wang et al. - 2025 - Fine-tuning a large multimodal neural network with clinical and dermoscopic images for accurate dete.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a fine-tuned large multimodal neural network based on the CLIP (contrastive language-image pretraining) model to classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and predict its histopathological subtypes using clinical and dermoscopic images. The method included an information complementary block (ICB) with ResNet feature extraction, logit-adjusted (LA) loss for long-tail distribution, and gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) for visualization.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the fine-tuning of a large multimodal neural network (CLIP-based) on clinical and dermoscopic images to detect basal cell carcinoma and predict subtypes. It describes specific methods (information complementary block, logit-adjusted loss), datasets (2675 and 594 images), results (accuracy of 0.899, sensitivity/specificity >0.90), and validation, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Fine-tuning a large multimodal neural network with clinical and dermoscopic images for accurate detection and histopathological subtype prediction of basal cell carcinoma in long-tailed distributions
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve the detection of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and prediction of its histopathological subtypes by fine-tuning a large multimodal neural network based on the CLIP model using clinical and dermoscopic images. The researchers constructed two datasets comprising 2675 images for BCC versus benign tumor classification and 594 images for distinguishing six BCC subtypes, addressing long-tail distribution issues with logit-adjusted loss. The proposed architecture integrated an information complementary block with ResNet feature extraction to fuse features from shallow and deep layers. Evaluation results demonstrated near-perfect sensitivity and specificity for the four-class task and an overall accuracy of 0.899 for the six-class subtype prediction task. The multimodal model outperformed single-modal models, effectively distinguishing nodular, superficial, and mixed BCC subtypes with high sensitivity and specificity. The findings suggest that this multimodal approach provides a robust tool for accurate BCC classification and subtype prediction in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for detection and histopathological subtype prediction. Subtypes studied include nodular (NOD), superficial (SUP), mixed (MIX), micronodular (MIC), infiltrative (INF), and basosquamous carcinoma (BSQ). Morpheaform BCC and fibroepithelial BCC were mentioned as excluded rare cases.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus benign tumors (Melanocytic Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma) and Multiclass classification of six BCC histopathological subtypes (Nodular, Superficial, Mixed, Micronodular, Infiltrative, Basosquamous).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes two classification tasks: a four-class task (BCC vs. benign) and a six-class task (BCC subtype distinction). The model uses a linear classification head for multiclass output. Performance is reported via accuracy (0.899 for six-class), sensitivity, specificity (>0.90 for NOD, SUP, MIX), and AUC/ROC curves. The task addresses long-tail distribution using logit-adjusted loss.

datasets_used.name: Peking Union Medical College Hospital Skin Imaging Database
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of clinical and dermoscopic images collected from the skin imaging database of the Department of Dermatology at Peking Union Medical College Hospital, China. Images were captured from June 2018 to January 2024, screened, and annotated by two experienced dermatologists.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the CLIP-based multimodal neural network for the four-class classification task (BCC vs. benign tumors).
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance on the four-class classification task.

datasets_used.name: Peking Union Medical College Hospital BCC Subtype Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the private dataset from Peking Union Medical College Hospital, specifically comprising BCC cases divided into six histopathological subtypes. This dataset exhibited a pronounced long-tail distribution.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the CLIP-based multimodal neural network for the six-class BCC subtype prediction task.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance on the six-class subtype prediction task.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address long-tail dataset imbalance by adjusting Softmax output probabilities based on category distribution and increasing focus on underrepresented categories.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves and Area under the curve (AUC)

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, ROC curves, AUC, and confusion matrices.

Six-class BCC subtype prediction task.dataset: Peking Union Medical College Hospital BCC Subtype Dataset (594 images)
Six-class BCC subtype prediction task.notes: The model demonstrated better performance in discerning nodular (NOD), superficial (SUP), and mixed (MIX) BCC, with sensitivity and specificity higher than 0.90. Performance in distinguishing micronodular (MIC) and infiltrative (INF) subtypes and basosquamous carcinoma (BSQ) was weaker. ROC curves and AUC results are shown in Figure 1A.

architecture_used.main_model: Large multimodal neural network based on the contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) model

architecture_details.fusion_module: Information complementary block (ICB) that fuses features from shallow to deep layers
architecture_details.feature_stages_used: Features from Stages 2 and 4 of ResNet
architecture_details.alignment_method: Contrastive loss to align image and category features

training_details.loss_function: Logit-adjusted (LA) loss (to address long-tail dataset imbalance) and contrastive loss
training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Freezing most parameters and selectively updating image encoder parameters

Single-modal models based on dermoscopic images
conclusions: The proposed multimodal neural network, fine-tuned on clinical and dermoscopic images using a CLIP-based architecture with an information complementary block (ICB) and logit-adjusted (LA) loss, achieved near-perfect sensitivity and specificity for distinguishing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from benign tumors (melanocytic nevus, seborrheic keratosis, and dermatofibroma).
The multimodal model outperformed single-modal models based on either clinical or dermoscopic images alone, demonstrating the robustness and superior reliability of integrating multiple modalities.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.899 in predicting six histopathological subtypes of BCC, with sensitivity and specificity higher than 0.90 for nodular, superficial, and mixed subtypes.
The model's performance was weaker in distinguishing micronodular, infiltrative subtypes, and basosquamous carcinoma, likely due to the long-tail distribution of these classes in the dataset.

The study acknowledges limitations including the exclusion of rare BCC subtypes (morpheaform and fibroepithelial BCC) and other skin tumors (hemangioma and melanoma), as well as the lack of external validation and real-world clinical setting evaluation.
Future work requires validation with external data, inclusion of more skin tumor types, and evaluation in real-world clinical settings to assess the model's application value.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation and real-world clinical setting evaluation, limiting generalizability.
The dataset exhibits a pronounced long-tail distribution, resulting in weaker performance for underrepresented subtypes (micronodular, infiltrative, and basosquamous BCC).
The study excludes rare BCC subtypes (morpheaform and fibroepithelial BCC) and other skin tumors (hemangioma and melanoma), restricting the scope of clinical applicability.
The paper is a correspondence/short report with limited detailed statistical reporting in the main text (e.g., specific AUC values are in supplementary materials).
The dataset consists solely of images from Chinese patients, potentially introducing population bias regarding skin type and lesion presentation.
No data leakage risks were explicitly reported, but the use of a private institutional database limits independent reproducibility.
